title: Chapter 9 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Class levels, Inheritance, and Modules!

questions.question: What is the primary difference between an Instance Method and a Class Method?
options: A Class Method belongs strictly to the Class Blueprint itself and can be called without creating an object.
A Class Method can only be used once.
An Instance method belongs to the Class Blueprint.
questions.explanation: You call Instance Methods on the objects you build (`fido.bark`), but you call Class Methods directly on the blueprint (`Dog.bark`).

questions.question: Which prefix physically glues a method securely to the Class blueprint rather than to its future Instances?
questions.explanation: The keyword `self` acts as a mirror reflecting the class. `def self.speak` means the method belongs strictly to the class itself.

questions.question: A Class Method can seamlessly access a specific object's `@name` instance variable.
questions.explanation: Variables starting with exactly one `@` are INSTANCE variables. Class methods are blind to them because they belong to the blueprint, not to any of the 100 individual dogs you might build.

questions.question: What prefix explicitly denotes a Class Variable that is securely shared across every single instance built from the Class?
questions.explanation: Remember: `@` is for private Instance data. `@@` is for globally shared Class data.

questions.question: Why do professional developers generally avoid using `@@` Class Variables?
options: If you update them in a subclass, it accidentally overwrites them in the parent class and every other subclass too, causing massive bugs.
questions.explanation: Because they are strictly global to the entire class family tree, changing them in a child permanently changes them for the parent and all siblings.

questions.question: Which mathematical symbol is perfectly tasked with defining class Inheritance in Ruby?
questions.explanation: If you read `class Dog < Animal` out loud, you can think of it as 'Dog is a sub-category of Animal'.

questions.question: If you build a massive `Vehicle` class containing a `drive` method, which of these is the most logical Child class?
questions.explanation: Inheritance requires a strict 'Is-A' relationship. A Motorcycle 'is a' Vehicle. Wheels and Engines 'belong to' a Vehicle.

questions.question: How do you forcefully override an inherited method from a Parent class?
options: You physically delete the method entirely from the parent class.
You just define a brand new method inside the Child class that has the exact same name.
questions.explanation: Ruby perfectly natively looks at the Child class first. If it finds the method there, it runs it immediately without checking the Parent.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the `super` keyword inside an overridden method?
options: It literally summons and runs the Parent's original version of the method right there, flawlessly merging the behaviors.
It turns the method into a Class Method.
questions.explanation: Using `super` allows a Child class to stack its own custom logic right on top of the Parent's existing logic.

questions.question: When `super` is called without any parentheses or arguments `()`, what happens to the arguments that were passed into the child method?
options: They are automatically forwarded perfectly into the Parent method.
questions.explanation: By default, calling naked `super` automatically feeds all received arguments straight up into the parent's method.

questions.question: What is a Module?
options: A class that can be instantiated with `.new`.
A bundle of methods that cannot be instantiated, designed specifically to be shared horizontally.
questions.explanation: A Module is like a mini-class that cannot be instantiated. Its sole purpose is to hold methods that can be 'mixed into' any other class.

questions.question: Which explicit keyword perfectly sucks a Module's methods seamlessly into a Class?
questions.explanation: The `include` keyword seamlessly copies-and-pastes all the methods securely from the Module directly into the Class's logic.

questions.question: In Ruby, a Class can inherit from multiple Parent classes using comma separation: `class Child < Mother, Father`.
questions.explanation: Ruby STRICTLY enforces Single Inheritance. A class can only have exactly ONE Parent class. However, it can `include` unlimited Modules.

questions.question: Because Modules are mixed into classes to provide behavior, the slang developer term for an included module is a _____.
questions.explanation: When you `include` a Module, you are 'mixing in' its functionality. Thus, they are universally called Mixins.

questions.question: If you need a `Duck` class to inherit `Animal` genetics, but also have `Flyable` and `Swimmable` abilities, how would you structure it?
options: Inherit from `Animal`, and `include` the `Flyable` and `Swimmable` modules.
questions.explanation: You get one Parent class for core identity (`class Duck < Animal`), but limitless Modules for versatile shared behaviors (`include Flyable`).

questions.question: If you do `fido = Dog.new`, what kind of method is `.new`?
questions.explanation: Because you are calling it directly on the `Dog` blueprint itself (and not on `fido`), it must be a Class Method!

questions.question: What is the difference between `include` and `extend` when using Modules?
options: `include` gives methods to the Instances. `extend` gives methods to the Class Blueprint itself.
`extend` gives methods to the Instances. `include` gives methods to the Class Blueprint.
`include` is for variables, `extend` is for methods.
questions.explanation: `include` brings the module's methods in as Instance Methods. `extend` brings them in as Class Methods (`self.method`).

questions.question: If `Fish < Animal` and `Shark < Fish`, does `Shark` have methods from `Animal`?
options: No, Inheritance only goes down one level.
Yes, Inheritance perfectly cascades infinitely down the family tree.
questions.explanation: Inheritance chains. A Shark inherits from Fish, which inherited from Animal. The Shark owns everything from the whole chain.

questions.question: What does a Module and a Class have in common?
options: They both act as incredibly neat containers to securely hold methods and logic.
questions.explanation: They both organize code wonderfully, but only a Class can be instantiated to create physical Objects.

questions.question: Look at: `Math.sqrt(9)`. Based on how it's called, what type of method is `sqrt`?
questions.explanation: You didn't build a `new` Math object. You called it directly on the giant capitalized `Math` namespace, meaning it's a Class/Module level method!
